items.myth: If I have good unit tests I don't need integration tests.
items.reality: Unit tests deliberately mock everything that crosses a boundary. The most common production bugs live at exactly those boundaries — schema drift, retry storms, contract mismatches. Without integration tests, that whole layer is invisible.
items.example: Migration changed `tags TEXT` → `tags JSONB`. Unit tests still pass (mocks don't care). Production breaks on first deploy.

items.myth: Integration tests are just slow unit tests.
items.reality: They have *different goals*. Unit tests verify individual logic; integration tests verify that components agree on contracts (types, schemas, error codes, ordering). Asking 'is this unit a unit test?' misses the point of the integration layer.
items.example: A 5-line integration test exercising the real DB + real ORM catches what 1000 mocked unit tests can't.

items.myth: Containers make integration tests fast and reliable.
items.reality: They make them *feasible*. Real reliability still requires pinned versions, per-test fresh state, careful parallelism, and proper teardown. Containers are necessary, not sufficient.
items.example: `postgres:latest` plus shared schemas across tests = flaky CI regardless of how nice testcontainers is.

items.reality: They drift the moment the upstream API changes. Re-record on a schedule (e.g. monthly) and run a small live-smoke suite to detect drift early.
items.example: OpenAI deprecates `gpt-4-turbo`. Your cassette still 'works' — but production calls fail. Smoke tests catch it; cassettes alone don't.

items.myth: More integration tests is always better.
items.reality: Integration tests are expensive — slower, flakier, more maintenance. The pyramid says *many* unit tests and *some* integration tests. Past a point, more integration tests slow the team without proportional coverage gain.
items.example: Aim for: lots of unit tests, ~10–50 integration tests covering critical paths, ~5–10 smoke tests against live deps. Tune for your domain.
